1: For Zion's sake I will not keep silent, and for Jerusalem's sake I will not be still, until her righteousness goes out like brightness and her salvation like a burning torch. The divine resolve to not-keep-silent is the prophetic mission statement: YHWH will speak and act until the full promise is realized. The brightness-going-out and the burning-torch-of-salvation are the missionary images: the light does not stay local but goes out. In the NT, Paul expresses the same apostolic compulsion: it has always been my ambition to preach the gospel where Christ was not known (Romans 15:20); I am obligated both to Greeks and non-Greeks (Romans 1:14). The light-going-out-from-Zion is the Pentecost mission: from Jerusalem, then to Judea, then Samaria, then the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8).
2: The nations will see your righteousness, and all the kings will see your glory; and you will be called by a new name that the mouth of YHWH will designate. The new-name promise is the covenant-identity transformation: what was called Forsaken and Desolate (v 4) will receive a YHWH-given name that reflects the restored relationship. In the NT, the new-name theology appears in Revelation 2:17 (I will give him a white stone with a new name written on it, known only to the one who receives it), Revelation 3:12 (I will write on him the name of my God and the new Jerusalem and my own new name), and the renaming pattern of Peter (Simon → Rock), Paul (Saul → Paul), and the community called Christians (Acts 11:26). The mouth-of-YHWH-designates the name in Isa 62 is fulfilled in the Spirit who gives new identity at baptism: you are now called by the name of Christ.
3: You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of YHWH, and a royal diadem in the palm of your God. The crown-of-beauty in YHWH's hand is the reversal of the fading crown of Ephraim (Isaiah 28:1): what was a crown of pride that fell is now a crown of beauty held securely in YHWH's own hand. In the NT, the royal-diadem imagery applied to the community appears in 1 Peter 2:9 (a royal priesthood) and Revelation 4:10-11 (the twenty-four elders cast their crowns before the throne). The crown held in YHWH's hand is the community kept by his power — the same keeping that Jesus promises: no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28).
4: You will no longer be called Forsaken, and your land will no longer be called Desolate; but you will be called My-Delight-Is-in-Her (Hephzibah), and your land Married (Beulah), for YHWH delights in you and your land will be married. The name-replacement is the theological heart of the chapter: the two names of exile-judgment (Forsaken, Desolate) are replaced by two names of covenant-restoration (My-Delight-Is-in-Her, Married). The marriage metaphor for covenant relationship — YHWH as husband, Zion as wife — is the Hosea-Ezekiel-Jeremiah theology applied eschatologically. In the NT: the church as the bride of Christ (Ephesians 5:25-32: Christ loved the church and gave himself for her); the New Jerusalem as the bride adorned for her husband (Revelation 21:2) — no longer Forsaken (Matthew 27:46: my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) but Hephzibah: the Beloved community in whom the Father delights.
5: For as a young man marries a virgin, so your sons will marry you; and as the bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you. The double marriage image — sons marrying Zion (the community reconstituting the city) and YHWH as bridegroom rejoicing over the bride — is the most intimate covenant description in Isaiah. The bridegroom-rejoices image is the NT's wedding-feast metaphor: the kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding banquet for his son (Matthew 22:2); the friend of the bridegroom listens for the bridegroom's voice and is full of joy when he hears it — this joy is mine and it is now complete (John 3:29). The eschatological form is the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7-9): rejoice! For the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready.
6: On your walls, O Jerusalem, I have set watchmen; all day and all night they will never fall silent. You who call on YHWH, take no rest for yourselves. The watchmen on the walls are the intercessors whose prayer sustains the city — they give YHWH no rest (v 7) until he establishes Jerusalem. The prayer-without-ceasing of the watchmen is the NT's pray without ceasing (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and the persistent widow who gives the unjust judge no rest (Luke 18:1-8: always pray and do not give up). The watchmen who intercede for Zion are the NT community's prayer-watch: the Gethsemane command — watch and pray (Matthew 26:41) — is the Isaianic watchman's commission applied to the disciples.
7: And give him no rest until he establishes and until he makes Jerusalem a praise on earth. The persistent-until-established prayer is the eschatological intercession: the watchmen do not stop until the promise is fully realized. In the NT: your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as in heaven (Matthew 6:10) — the Lord's Prayer is the watchman's prayer of Isaiah 62:7. The until-he-establishes timeframe is the until-he-comes of the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 11:26: you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes) — the community maintains its watchful intercession until the full establishment of the Jerusalem-above (Hebrews 12:22, Revelation 21:2) arrives.
8: YHWH has sworn by his right hand and by his mighty arm: Surely I will no longer give your grain as food for your enemies, and foreigners will not drink your new wine for which you have toiled. The oath-by-his-right-hand is the highest form of divine guarantee: YHWH swears by himself (Hebrews 6:13: since there was no one greater for him to swear by, he swore by himself). The promise that the harvest will not be taken by enemies reverses the covenant-curse of Leviticus 26:16 and Deuteronomy 28:33 (a nation you do not know will eat what your land produces). In the NT, the harvest theology is eschatological: the fields are ripe for harvest (John 4:35) and the harvest is the end of the age (Matthew 13:39) — the grain that was taken by enemies will ultimately be gathered by the Lord of the harvest (Matthew 9:38).
9: But those who harvest it will eat it and praise YHWH, and those who gather it will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary. The restoration of the harvest-into-worship cycle: the eating-and-praising in the sanctuary courts is the covenant-feast that the earlier thefts had interrupted. In the NT: the eschatological feast where the redeemed eat in the Father's house (Luke 22:30: I confer on you a kingdom... you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom) is the sanctuary-courts meal of Isaiah 62:9 fulfilled. The Lord's Supper itself is the anticipatory participation in this feast: the church eats bread and drinks wine until the harvest-feast of the kingdom comes in its fullness.
10: Go through, go through the gates! Prepare the way for the people! Build up, build up the highway; clear it of stones; lift up a banner over the peoples. The highway-preparation oracle (echoing 40:3 and Isaiah 57:14) is the infrastructure of the eschatological homecoming: the road is built, the stones are cleared, the banner is raised. In the NT: John the Baptist is the voice preparing the way (Matthew 3:3 citing Isaiah 40:3) and the Great Commission is the raising of the banner over the peoples. The banner (nes) is the signal-flag that rallies the scattered peoples to YHWH's assembly — the NT's equivalent is the cross lifted up: when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself (John 12:32).
11: Behold, YHWH has proclaimed to the end of the earth: Say to Daughter Zion, See, your salvation comes! See, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him. The say-to-Daughter-Zion oracle is the announcement that Matthew applies to the Triumphal Entry: say to Daughter Zion, See, your king comes to you (Matthew 21:5, combining Isaiah 62:11 with Zechariah 9:9). The salvation that comes is Jesus himself — the name Jesus (Yeshua) means YHWH saves — riding into Jerusalem as the fulfillment of the Savior-who-comes announced here. His reward and recompense are the judgment-and-salvation he brings: his reward with him anticipates Revelation 22:12 (I am coming soon; my reward is with me, to give to each person according to what they have done).
12: And they shall be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of YHWH; and you shall be called Sought Out, a City Not Forsaken. The four new names at the chapter's end are the covenant-identity of the restored community: Holy People, Redeemed of YHWH, Sought Out, City Not Forsaken. In the NT: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession (1 Peter 2:9) — the Holy People; the Redeemed by Christ's blood (1 Peter 1:18-19); Sought Out by the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7); Not Forsaken: I will never leave you nor forsake you (Hebrews 13:5). The four names of Isaiah 62:12 are the NT community's four-fold identity granted by the Savior who came to Daughter Zion (v 11).
